div.h2: Stress Test Architecture: 4 Writers × 4 Readers + Checksum Verification
div.p: concurrent_test.py — NUM_WRITERS=4 · NUM_READERS=4 · MESSAGES_PER_WRITER=50 · MESSAGE_SIZE=128 bytes

Why the test is reliable: Race conditions are probabilistic — a single writer rarely exposes the bug. 4 concurrent writers × 50 messages × 128-byte blocks provides thousands of opportunities for CPU interleaving. On a multi-core machine, missing-mutex corruption typically manifests within the first 20 messages. The test is not exhaustive (no formal proof), but it is a high-confidence detector in practice.

Race condition without mutex: CPU 0 (Writer A): CPU 1 (Writer B): to_copy = 128 to_copy = 128 copy_from_user(buf, "AAA…") copy_from_user(buf, "BBB…") buffer_data_len += 128 buffer_data_len += 128 ← LOST UPDATE Reader sees 128 bytes of interleaved A+B bytes. SHA-256 of mixed block ∉ sent_checksums → FAIL
msg = b'A' * 128 cksum = sha256(msg) → sent_checksums.put(cksum) × 50 iterations
msg = b'B' * 128 cksum = sha256(msg) → sent_checksums.put(cksum) × 50 iterations
msg = b'C' * 128 cksum = sha256(msg) → sent_checksums.put(cksum) × 50 iterations
msg = b'D' * 128 cksum = sha256(msg) → sent_checksums.put(cksum) × 50 iterations

FIFO buffer — shared mutable state kernel_buffer[0 .. buffer_data_len) buffer_data_len ← bytes currently stored buffer_size = 4096 Protected by: mydevice_mutex Readers sleep on: mydevice_read_queue Writers sleep on: mydevice_write_queue
Critical section boundary Every read() and write() acquires this before touching kernel_buffer or buffer_data_len Missing mutex → lost update: buffer_data_len torn between CPUs
data = os.read(fd, 128) cksum = sha256(data) → append to received_checksums loop until stop_event
data = os.read(fd, 128) cksum = sha256(data) → append to received_checksums
data = os.read(fd, 128) cksum = sha256(data) → append to received_checksums
data = os.read(fd, 128) cksum = sha256(data) → append to received_checksums
Populated by: all 4 writers Contents: SHA-256 hex strings Total entries: 4 × 50 = 200 Each entry = sha256(b'X' * 128) Only 4 distinct values exist (one per writer character)

Populated by: all 4 readers Contents: SHA-256 of each 128-byte block actually read from the device If mutex correct: every entry ∈ sent_checksums set If mutex missing: corrupted blocks produce unknown SHA-256 → ∉ sent_checksums → FAIL

corrupted = [c for c in received_checksums if c not in expected_set] len(corrupted) == 0 Every 128-byte block read back is exactly one writer's pure message: all-A, all-B, all-C, or all-D. Conclusion: mutex serialises writes correctly — no data interleaving.

len(corrupted) > 0 At least one 128-byte block contains bytes from two different writers: e.g., first 64 bytes = 'A', last 64 = 'B' sha256(mixed_block) is NOT in {sha256(b'A'*128), sha256(b'B'*128), …} Conclusion: lost update / torn write → buffer_data_len corrupted.
